The Agile Mind/LEAF Lab

An Agile operating system for learning.

Students plan their own work with MoSCoW boards and daily standups. Teachers see exactly where a lesson breaks down. Every task maps to a measurable life skill.

See the work My role
RoleProduct Manager Intern
OrgLEAF Lab
Built withFigmaMake
SurfacesStudent + Instructor
Framework24 life skills

The idea

Teach the skills school skips.

School teaches subjects but skips the skills that decide how far those subjects go: planning, focus, resilience, communication. The Agile Mind builds 24 of those life skills into daily learning. Students plan and reflect with the same Agile tools software teams use. Teachers get a clear view of where learning stalls. Every task ties to a skill, so growth is something everyone can see.

24
life skills tagged across every task in TAM
11 pts
higher academic achievement for students in social-emotional learning programs
7 in 10
employers call human skills like analytical thinking and resilience essential today
39%
of core job skills will change by 2030

Sources: Durlak et al. (2011) social-emotional learning meta-analysis of 213 programs; World Economic Forum Future of Jobs 2025.

My role

I owned the product, not the code.

I directed the product and designed the experience. The working prototype was generated with FigmaMake from my specifications, then refined screen by screen.

  • directedDirected the product across four role-based views, deciding what each role needed to see and do.
  • designedDesigned the student experience around Agile habits: a MoSCoW board, daily standup check-ins, a focus timer, confidence ratings, and weekly reflection.
  • shapedShaped the instructor portal into six tabs that follow a teacher's day, then specced two systems: Content Health and a teacher MoSCoW workspace.
  • coordinatedCoordinated the work in Kanban Zone, planning what shipped next and keeping coaches, partner teams, and stakeholders aligned.
  • mappedMapped every task and assignment to a 24 life-skill framework, so growth stays visible to students, teachers, and parents at once.
  • wroteWrote the build specifications that FigmaMake turned into the prototype, and iterated from screenshots rather than guesswork.

Surface one

The student workbook.

Students run their day like a small Agile team. They check in, prioritize, focus, and reflect. The tools are real, not simplified, which is the point.

Dashboard. A daily standup check-in, streaks and XP, and today's focus task with an AI coach that asks rather than answers.

MoSCoW board. Students sort their own work into Must, Should, Could, and Won't, the same method professional product teams use.

Weekly reflection. What went well, what was hard, what they learned, and a goal for next week.

Surface two

The instructor portal.

Teachers get the other half: visibility into students, and tools to manage their own workload. The nav runs left to right through a teacher's day.

Dashboard. Course snapshots, class-wide skill growth, and a quick read on who needs support and who is thriving.

Teacher workspace. A MoSCoW grading board, so teachers model the same prioritization they teach students.

Content Health. Flags the exact lessons students get stuck on, with a specific read on why, not a generic alert.

Signature system

Content Health escalates on its own.

When students get stuck on the same material, the system speaks up before a teacher goes looking. It fires once at each threshold, and each level replaces the last.

2
Watch

Two students stuck. A quiet heads-up that something is worth keeping an eye on.

3
Urgent

Three students hitting the same wall. A content signal, not a student one.

5
Critical

Five students stuck. This needs revision before more students hit the wall. Snooze only, no permanent dismiss.

A second system links grading to planning. When a graded assignment shows the class missing the same concept, the board generates a suggested re-teach card. When grading and Content Health point at the same concept, the suggestion jumps straight to Must Teach. The teacher stays in control of every card.

The framework

Five skill pillars, one system.

The 24 skills group into five pillars, each with its own product and color. The color system runs through the whole platform.

Work
The Blueprint
Grades 6–12
Think
Think Sideways
All ages
Practice
One Step
Ages 8–11
Plan
Dream.Plan.Do.
Grades K–5
Study
Deep Study
Grades 6–12

What it proved

Key takeaways.

Agile scales beyond software

Standups, MoSCoW, and retrospectives work for students, not just engineering teams. Real tools build real habits.

Visibility drives accountability

Tying every task to 24 skills made growth measurable for students, teachers, and parents at the same time.

Ownership builds confidence

Structured autonomy over their own work built student confidence, resilience, and independence.

Teacher well-being matters

Content Health and the teacher workspace treat teacher workload as a real problem, not an afterthought.
